title: Reversible fully spin polarization in strain-engineered two-dimensional fully compensated magnets
authors: Xiuli Zhang, Peng Jiang, Yurui Ma, Xiaodong Zhou, Linlin Liu, Hong-Mei Huang, San-Dong Guo, Tengfei Cao, Yan-Ling Li
abstract: Achieving controllable spin polarization and its reversal in symmetry-compensated magnets. Here we demonstrate, using symmetry analysis and a minimal tight-binding model, that uniaxial strain removes these constraints by inducing inequivalence between magnetic sublattices in two-dimensional (2D) system, driving an altermagnetic (AM) state into a fully compensated ferrimagnetic (fFIM) state and enabling fully spin polarization. Furthermore, strain along orthogonal directions gives rise to two energetically degenerate fFIM states with opposite spin polarization, enabling reversible spin switching. More importantly, the two symmetry-related fFIM states can be regarded as distinct ferroelastic variants, suggesting that this model or mechanism can be extended to ferroelastic fFIM systems. The generality of this mechanism is confirmed by combining spin-group analysis, first-principles calculations, and Boltzmann transport theory in representative candidates, including AM Mn$_2$SeO and ferroelastic fFIM V$_2$SO. Our results reveal a universal symmetry-driven framework for strain-controlled and -reversible fully spin-polarized transport and identify strain-engineered AM and ferroelastic fFIM systems as a promising platform for volatile and nonvolatile spintronic applications.
summary: This paper demonstrates that applying uniaxial strain to 2D altermagnets can break magnetic symmetries to induce a fully spin-polarized ferrimagnetic state. By controlling the direction of the strain, researchers can reversibly switch the spin polarization, providing a new framework for developing efficient, strain-controlled spintronic devices.
main_problem: The difficulty of achieving controllable and reversible full spin polarization in symmetry-compensated magnets, such as altermagnets, where crystalline symmetries enforce spin degeneracy.
main_result: Uniaxial strain can break the symmetry between magnetic sublattices, driving a transition from an altermagnetic state to a fully compensated ferrimagnetic state that enables 100% spin polarization and reversible spin switching.
method: The study uses symmetry and spin-group analysis, a minimal tight-binding model, first-principles DFT calculations, Boltzmann transport theory, and ab initio molecular dynamics.
model_or_system: The study focuses on 2D altermagnetic (AM) and ferroelastic fully compensated ferrimagnetic (fFIM) systems, specifically identifying monolayer Mn2SeO and V2SO as candidate materials.
key_observables: Spin polarization percentage, spin-polarized band structure, integrated density of states (IDOS), spin-dependent electronic occupation, and spin-resolved longitudinal conductivities.
important_parameters: Uniaxial strain (compressive/tensile), strain direction (orthogonal x and y), and the transition from AM to fFIM states.
main_assumptions: The mechanism is assumed to be general for 2D systems where uniaxial strain can induce sublattice inequivalence.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the illustration of strain engineering and Brillouin zones; Fig 2 displays the atomic structure, spin density, and spin-polarized transport for Mn2SeO; Fig 3 demonstrates the reversal of spin polarization in Mn2SeO under different strain directions; Fig 4 compares the ferroelastic configurations of V2SO.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of spin degeneracy in compensated magnets, presents a symmetry-based mechanism using a tight-binding model, validates the mechanism with first-principles calculations on Mn2SeO and V2SO, and concludes with the implications for spintronic applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The illustration of uniaxial strain engineering in 2D AM and fFIM systems. (b) First Brillouin zone (BZ) of 2D square and rectangular lattices. (c) Schematic of a 2D two-sublattice AM model and (d) its corresponding spin-polarized band structure and IDOS with ∆= 0 and tx A = ty B = 0.1t, and ty A = tx B = −0.2t, where sublattices A and B are located at (0.0, 0.5) and (0.5, 0.0), respectively, carrying opposite magnetic moments and interconnected by [C2∥O] (O= C4z or Mxy) symmetry, in the absence of t and P symmetries. (e, g) Schematics of a 2D AM model under compressive strain along the x and y directions, also corresponding to ferroelastic fFIM states FE1 (a < b) and FE2 (a >...

FIG. 2. (a) Atomic structure, spin density, and (b) spin-polarized band structure of the 2D ML-Mn2SeO. (c) Spin-resolved longitu- dinal conductivities and (d) spin polarization (SP) as a function of the energy in ML-Mn2SeO along x and y directions. The light gray shaded area represents the band gap region. EF is set to the energy of the VBM.

FIG. 3. (a) Band structure and IDOS, (b) spin-resolved conductivity as a function of energy, and (c) energy-dependent spin polarization along the x and y directions for Mn2SeO under 5% compressive strain along the x axis (εx = -5%). (d)–(f) The corresponding results under 5% compressive strain along the y axis (εy = -5%), shown for direct comparison and highlighting the strain-induced spin reversal. The light gray and pink shaded areas represent the band gap and spin-splitting gap regions, respectively.

FIG. 4. (a) Crystal structure and spin density, (b) spin polarized band structure and IDOS, (c) spin-resolved conductivity, and (d) spin polarization SP along the x and y directions for the FE1 phase of V2SO. (e)–(h) The corresponding quantities for the FE2 phase, shown in the same sequence for direct comparison. The light gray and pink shaded areas represent the band gap and spin-splitting gap regions, respectively.
